data.title: Citizens Financial: Solid Q1 Shows No Signs Of Economic Weakness
data.description:
We are early in our coverage of the Q1 regional bank earnings season. We believe reviewing the earnings of regional banks is a great way to do a "pulse check" on the strength of the economy both locally and regionally. Thus far we have seen strength in the few that have reported thus far.

In today's column, we return to Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG). We maintain a neutral rating at these levels, and note that operations are remaining resilient. Citizens is one of the larger regional banks that we cover. A few years ago management embarked on a strategy to be more strict in its lending standards and this paid off with improving asset quality over the last few years. While there was some pressure on loan volume from the tightening of standards, the margin power has gotten better. Further, the bank stock continues to raise its dividend, with another 9.5% increase announced last fall. Today Citizens just-reported Q1 earnings. Let us check in on and discuss the key metrics of Citizens Financial that we follow.

Citizens Financial Q1 Earnings Headline Results

In the just-reported Q1, Citizens saw positive results overall. Revenues rose a robust 11.9% to $2.17 billion from a year ago. It was essentially in line with consensus expectations (a beat of $10 million). The provision for credit losses was $140 million, which was down from $153 million from last year's Q1. We see no issue with this level of provisions which could indicate issues with repayment or expected losses. In fact, loan loss provisions for losses have been around the same level each quarter over the last year and are edging lower over time. That is a good sign.

The bank saw net income increase from $373 million last year to $517 million in Q1. It did however edge slightly lower from $528 million in the sequential Q4. But EPS was better than expected, It hit $1.13 and was a $0.04 beat against consensus expectations. This was up 47% from last year last year and was in line with the sequential Q4. As noted above, with tightening standards, deposits and loans had declined for many quarters. But they are now stabilizing and moving in the right direction.

Citizens Financial Q1 Loans and Deposits

This quarter, average deposits were up to $181.3 billion versus $179.9billion in the sequential quarter. They were also up from $172.7 billion a year ago. So, it seems the long decline in deposits has stopped and now reversed over the last year. This is very positive news, as lending standards remain strict. That strict lending criteria drove total loans down for many quarters, but loans have started to increase again over the last year. That said, loans at period-end were $143.7 million in Q1, up from $142.7 million starting the quarter. Here is the recent trend:

This is positive. Growth in loans and deposits is key for any regional bank that we cover, we now have a return to growth.

Citizens Financial Q1 Asset Quality and Return Metrics

Asset quality has stabilized as well, and 2025 as a whole enjoyed a nice trend for asset quality. We want to point out that net interest margin of 3.14% was up 8 basis points from the sequential Q4, which was positive. Margins back above 3% are a huge improvement. Net interest margin was up 25 basis points from a year ago. This is helping drive earnings strength.

Net loan charge-offs are also trending lower, and that is a sign of strength. These were $138 million, $17 million lower than in the sequential Q4, and down from $175 million a year ago. Thus, net charge-offs were just 0.39% as a percentage of all loans, the lowest it has been in a year.

This is a solid trend folks. Nonaccrual loans were $1.5 billion and were down modestly from the sequential Q4. There was improvement in commercial, largely C&I, with minor increases in retail loans, largely mortgages.

So better loan balances, higher deposits, and improving credit metrics. This is a sign of strength. One minor blemish was that the efficiency ratio softened to 63.6% from 62.2% in the sequential Q4. But this is an improvement from last year's 67.9%.

Finally, the return metrics are in strong shape and held mostly firm from the sequential quarter. The underlying return on average tangible assets was 0.97% vs. 0.98% in the sequential quarter and was up nicely from 0.73% last year. The underlying return on average equity hit 12.19% versus 12.18% in the sequential Q4, holding firm at this elevated level. Notably it was up from 9.64% a year ago.

Final thoughts

Citizens Financial Group, Inc's strategy to tighten up lending standards a few years ago led to a number of quarters of adjustment. But it is back in growth mode. We are seeing increases in loan balances, higher deposits, and improving credit metrics. Return metrics are as good as they have been in years. We see no signs of economic weakness in this report. Overall, we view CFG stock as a quality Hold.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors Q1 Preview: Auto Disappoints, But A New Growth Driver Is Coming
data.description:
NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NXPI) is due to report its Q1 ’26 earnings on the 28th of April, so I thought I’d look into what to expect and what I am looking for to give us a better look at the company’s potential and whether it would be a good time to start a position.

Since the last article on the company, its share price declined by around 9% against 2% gain for the SPY (SPY). I don’t see anything particularly exciting yet, so I maintain my Hold rating until we hear more about the next big growth driver.

What to Expect

Analysts are expecting the company to make around $2.98 and $3.52 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, which is the opposite of what we usually see with these numbers. Additionally, they expect the company to make $3.15B in revenues, which translates to around 11% growth y/y, but around 6% decline sequentially. It is worth noting that there have been 14 upward and 6 downward revisions to the EPS over the last 90 days. These numbers are in line with what the management guided for the upcoming quarter.

Over the last ten quarters, the company only missed twice when it came to revenues and essentially met or beat EPS estimates 100% of the time. A miss by a penny is negligible.

I think there is a good chance that we will see the company beating these estimates once again because it looks like the management either consistently low-balls its guidance or the analysts are underestimating NXPI’s earnings potential. Either way, it’s a positive for the company. Judging by the historical evidence, the beat is not going to be mind-blowing, so I don’t expect a massive share price swing on the day.

So, what that means for my fellow options traders is that if you want to play any direction on the day, the implied move is around d 3.6%, which is quite weak. So, being a seller of options will most likely pay off if you sell far out of money strikes on an iron condor or a strangle, as IV crush will take care of the rest, but just be careful with strangles because you could get wrecked if the move is around 10% on the day. IV crush and theta decay will not be able to offset the increase in short gamma in that case. If you are looking to buy an out-of-money call or put option, it’s essentially a lottery ticket, so it is up to you. Always look for options that have enough volume, so the spread is tight.

What I’m looking for

The biggest item I will be looking out for in the upcoming earnings call is the move NXPI is making into Physical AI and robotics. This is a massive pivot into one of the more promising technology segments these days. This will no doubt give a decent boost to the company’s Industrial and IoT revenue segment, but since it is still in its early innings, I am not expecting miracles just yet. I want to hear some commentary regarding its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) for physical AI and what kind of TAM they are looking to push into.

NXP said that its new physical AI work is built on secure edge platforms, real-time networking, motor control, and machine vision, so the relevant TAM is much closer to semiconductor content in robotics than the full robot market value. If we look at the third-party data, outer TAM for semis in robotics stood at around $11.2B in 2025, which is expected to rise to a whopping $41.2B by 2030. That is a CAGR of 29.7%. Sure, this is very appealing as a growth factor; however, we need to be realistic here and not expect NXPI to capture a lot of that expansion. To be conservative, I am expecting very little in gaining market share in the next few quarters, mainly because the company is not going for the AI accelerators in the robots, but rather it is targeting the edge control stack, connectivity, motor control, and safety. So, the long-term TAM could be somewhere between $2B to $5B, and the number is closer to millions in the short to medium term outlook for NXPI.

Automotive remains the largest revenue contributor by far, and with the weakness that we have seen in this segment, I am really looking forward to the commentary when the management finally says that the segment is starting to see some strength after a long inventory correction. Unfortunately, that has not been the case just yet. The recovery is taking so much longer than expected, but at least the company managed to keep up the revenues at these levels.

With the upbeat guidance from the previous earnings report, many analysts agree that the automotive segment is stabilizing, but are still being quite conservative and do not dare call for an aggressive rebound.

Once again, we just have to hope that there will be a clear improvement in the segment by late 2026, if auto production steadies and restocking finally starts. I’ve been looking forward to this for a couple of years now, and this scenario just never came to fruition, so I am not going to be holding my breath this year. I really don’t expect a marked improvement yet. I rather hope that NXPI’s venture into physical AI is a lot more successful, which will help offset the lackluster top-line performance overall.

Am I looking to buy NXPI before earnings?

On paper, the company seems cheap, with a forward PE ratio of around 15; however, when you think about it, the company’s revenue segments aren’t growing particularly fast, as there seems to be some weakness across the board, especially its largest revenue segment, exhibiting consistently poor performance.

I think I will be holding off for now and waiting to see how the company’s venture into physical AI and robotics pans out. I will be very interested in hearing the potential in this segment from the management in the upcoming conference call. It would be amazing to see that there might be a promising growth segment added to the company’s operations, as long as NXPI can execute the plan to perfection.

data.title: First Horizon: A Decent Start, But Follow Through Is Key
data.description:
It’s still early in the quarterly earnings reporting cycle, and while First Horizon’s (FHN) business appears to be in solid shape, it is unlikely to be among the performance leaders for the quarter. Credit quality remains good and First Horizon still enjoys good share in the attractive Southeastern market, but soft loan growth is a watch item going forward given that spread margin growth is going to be challenging from here.

First Horizon shares are up about 12% since my last update on the bank, which is basically in line with the broader regional bank space and a little better than the average Southeastern bank of comparable size.

At this point I do think First Horizon shares offer some value in a space that doesn’t have a lot of obvious bargains. That said, higher energy prices are a threat to the economy both from the perspective of business activity/investment and rates, so I do see a risk of weaker loan growth across the sector. For investors who can be patient, today’s price still offers a respectable quality/value balance.

A Small Beat, But One That Brings Some Sustainability Questions

First Horizon did beat expectations for the first quarter, with core earnings that were three cents better than expected ($0.53 vs. $0.50) and about a penny better at the core pre-provision earnings line.

Revenue rose over 6% year over year and contracted about 2% quarter over quarter, coming in just a bit below sell-side expectations. Net interest income improved about 6% yoy and contracted 1% qoq, about 1% better than expected (driving a $0.01/share beat), with weaker than expected earning assets (up 3% yoy, almost flat qoq) offset by a stronger than expected net interest margin (up 10bp yoy / 1 bp qoq to 3.52%) that in turn was driven by lower than expected deposit/funding costs.

Fee-based income growth was a little disappointing, growing 9% yoy and contracting 5% qoq and missing by about 5% (or about $0.015/share). Weaker service charges were the main driver relative to sell-side expectations, and it appears that this result was more a byproduct of quarterly “noise” than any fundamental issues.

Core expenses (including amortization) rose less than 4% yoy and contracted more than 7% qoq, coming in about 2% lower than expected and beating by about $0.015/share.

Pre-provision profits rose almost 11% yoy and 6% qoq, beating by about 2%. The ratio of PPOP to average earning assets improved 13bp from the year-ago period, but at about 1.88%, there’s still room for improvement (“good” usually starts at over 2%).

Below the PPOP line, lowering provision expense drove almost $0.03/share of earnings upside, while taxes and share count were both a bit higher than Street expectations.

Weaker Balance Sheet Growth And Limited Rate Leverage Drive Sustainability Questions

The most disappointing part of First Horizon’s first quarter was the weaker-than-expected balance sheet growth. With likely limited ability to drive meaningful upside in NIM in the coming quarters, balance sheet growth is going to be a key driver for earnings growth.

Loans rose a little less than 3% yoy and contracted 0.4% qoq on an average balance basis, and that is likely to stack up as a bit soft compared to other regional banks, and particularly larger banks.

Elevated commercial real estate (or CRE) paydowns are definitely a headwind, but with management pointing to a multiyear high CRE pipeline, this could be abating in the coming quarters. A key here is the health of the multifamily construction market, as this is an important category of CRE lending for First Horizon, but several Southeastern markets have become overbuilt in recent quarters.

Core commercial lending growth of 8% yoy and 0.6% qoq was likewise a little disappointing. I want to see what other Southeastern-focused banks report, but it does appear that commercial lending has been stronger with multinational corporations than with smaller businesses, and that typically favors the largest banks like Bank of America (BAC), JPMorgan (JPM), PNC (PNC), et al over banks like First Horizon.

Mortgage warehouse lending was weak seasonally (down more than 6% qoq), but up about 38% yoy. This is more of a “coiled spring” sort of business for First Horizon. Unlike many banks, First Horizon didn’t bail out of this business when activity plunged on higher rates, and if or when rates move back below 6% for 30-year mortgages, a refi wave should drive significant growth and revenue/profits from this line.

Looking at rates, First Horizon benefited from a 25bp qoq decline in interest-bearing deposit costs, but spot rates have more or less flattened out and deposit competition could well pick up in the summer. At the same time, lending markets are competitive enough that there’s not much pricing power here without compromising credit quality, particularly with an influx of regional/super-regional banks into the Southeast lending market.

The sustainability of First Horizon’s growth really rests on balance sheet growth now. Management has caught up on balance sheet restructuring (deposit growth) in the wake of the failed Toronto-Dominion (TD) deal, and First Horizon enjoys a strong core deposit franchise in markets like Tennessee and North Carolina, but competition is picking up and a 95% loan/deposit ratio doesn’t leave a lot of wiggle room.

As I said above, a recovery in regional CRE activity would be a definite positive for the bank, as would a stronger recovery in small/mid-sized business lending. First Horizon has a strong franchise here in its core market with good customer relationships, but there’s not a lot that the bank can do if business uncertainty remains high and/or the Fed hikes rates in response to inflationary pressures.

The Outlook

Many of these macro challenges are largely outside of management’s direct control – while the bank can gain share in C&I lending and/or deposits in its core markets, overall demand is outside of their control. I do think increased M&A activity in the region should help the bank on the margin (hiring away loan officers and/or luring away customers), and I also expect a “slow and steady” effort to expand branches in already-strong markets like North Carolina and Tennessee.

M&A remains a key unknown and a potential source of volatility. A harshly negative reaction from the Street has led management to back away from prior comments about a willingness to look for deals, but I still think management would consider a tuck-in deal and/or branch acquisitions if the right opportunity emerged. At the same time, I think the bank’s strong fee-generating businesses (including bond trading and leasing) and strong lending/deposit franchise make it a credible M&A target for a super-regional looking to accelerate their Southeastern growth ambitions.

As far as modeling goes, good expense leverage remains a definite positive, and I do think loan growth will pick up provided that the U.S. economy isn’t dragged down by persistently higher energy prices. I’m a little above the Street for FY’26 EPS at $2.18 (vs. $2.14) but I expect that Street number will head higher as sell-side analysts publish updated models after the Q1 report.

I’m looking for core earnings growth of around 7% over the next five years and long-term growth closer to 5%. That supports a fair value of around $26/share. I get a similar number from my ROTCE-driven P/TBV and P/E approaches, with an actual range of $25.80 from ROTCE (assuming a 15% ROTCE) to $26.20 using a 12x multiple on my ’26 EPS estimate.

The Bottom Line

It’s certainly possible that the first quarter earnings reporting cycle will drive a broader set of model and fair value revisions, but at this point First Horizon looks like a relative bargain in the space. Upside of around 10% isn’t superb, but a lot of regional banks appear to offer even less, and many of the would-be bargains have operational issues (like First Citizens (FCNCA) and ongoing weakness in the VC market).

With a good management team, strong core franchises in attractive markets, and M&A optionality, not to mention a decent valuation, I think First Horizon remains a name worth considering for investors who still want exposure to regional banking.

data.title: Pulsar Helium: "Blue Gold" And Its Role In Quantum Computing
data.description:
Incorporated in 2022, Pulsar Helium (PSRHF) is an exploration and development company focused primarily on helium. Headquartered in Portugal, and registered in White Rock, Canada, the company is currently at work on a site just outside Babbitt, Minnesota in the Iron Range (its Topaz Project). Though it also has a 100% interest in a helium reservoir in Greenland and more recently one in Michigan, Topaz is its true lodestone and the company’s fortunes will be tied to its success there.

Pulsar has completed seven appraisal wells at the Topaz site. All seven "Jetstreams" have successfully yielded gas, and showed consistent (500-1290 psi) pressure, with “Jetstream #1” having helium levels at an astonishing 14.5%, well above normative commercial ranges (.3% - 1%) and three other wells –Jetstream 3,4,5-- reporting concentrations of 8% to 10%.

These are remarkable numbers, orders of magnitude better than existing world-class helium deposits, and it suggests a very lucrative opportunity with the right operational follow-through. Sproule ERCO –a well-established independent estimator in the field—has looked at the site and assessed (based on Jetstream #1) that Topaz has at least 380 million cubic feet of helium-4. A recent 40-mile seismic survey suggests that this substantial high-pressure helium-4 reservoir is 2 miles in depth and has at least 2.8 billion cubic feet of helium. (Jetstream #1 is more than 5,000 feet deep and #2 is around 5,600 ft).

These are impressive numbers, but company is still in its early, risky innings. Pulsar Helium is a pre-revenue E&P venture that has yet to make a dime. It is funneling everything into its wells and their commercialization. The firm reported a net loss of $9.65 million for FY 2025 (ending 9/30/25), with total assets of $4.2 million versus $1.5 million in liabilities reported. Short-term investments and cash were only $2.4 million, though in a mid-January presentation the company indicated it had about $10 million in cash.

In the most recent quarter (1Q 26), the firm recorded exploration expenditures of CAD$2 million. It issued 16.15 million warrants for CAD$4.1 million and 800,000 stock options for CAD$.3 million. Post period, in February, Pulsar raised around CAD$13.7 million via OAK Securities in London.

With a market cap of $253 million, Pulsar is a true micro-cap. Registered in British Columbia, it listed on the Toronto Exchange in 2023 (under the ticker PLSR) and has since been added to British (AIM) and US (OTC) exchanges.

The company is a tiny speculative ticker so investors should approach it accordingly, with dilution a key risk for companies this size. Share count essentially doubled over the past two years and may now be 197 million as of April 15, 2026. (Last month a director left, exercising 450,000 stock options, and two employees exercised 2000 stock options).

Management

The company does have an experienced management team. CEO Thomas Abraham-James is a highly regarded geologist (and Geological Society of London Fellow) with global expertise in helium exploration across several continents. The recent appointment of Cliff Cain as President –bringing experience in large-scale gas agreements and the US regulatory milieu (he has advised both the EPA and GSA)—will strengthen commercialization efforts.

Nick Schofield, Pulsar’s Chief Geologist, is a hard rock specialist in non-hydro-carbon bearing systems, while Peter Barry is the firm’s in-house noble-gas geochemist with wide ranging helium expertise. Both carry relevant experience for the Topaz endeavor.

In terms of M&A and operations: the company rolled up 80% of Quantum Hydrogen last fall (I am not sure why) and tapped big dog Chart Industries (GTLS) to build its processing plant (permitting expected next month). The plant was initiated last fall after a $12.5 million “expression of intent” from a local bank and major shareholder University Bancorp. (That bank’s CEO just joined the board, filling the seat made empty on March 31).

The Central Challenge

Can a “primary helium” outfit ever hope to compete against big natural gas providers which offer the substance as a dross by-product of their main operation? That is the central issue with this company. It was the cause of pessimism by an earlier SA analyst (see here).

That analyst was writing in 2024, however, well before firm’s appraisal wells were completed, and --more importantly-- before discovery of helium-3 at Topaz. In January, both the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory and the US Geological Survey Labs confirmed helium-3 concentrations of 11.2-1.9 ppb at Jetstream #1 –among the highest levels ever recorded in a natural gas reservoir.

Helium-3 is extremely rare and valuable, with prices between $20 million per kilogram $2500 per liter) –vastly higher than the more common helium-4 (presently $69.57/kg). As a super-light, stable isotope with one neutron and two protons, He-3 is astonishingly unique as a substance in the wild.

Its scarcity has historically limited its usage, with most supply derived from tritium decay created in labs. However, demand is expanding rapidly, particularly due to advanced technologies. That reduced scope was okay when the isotope’s central use case was in border security (for neutron detectors that can catch radioactive materials at ports of entry). At present, however, there is an enormous new demand coming from quantum computing. It is an emerging market, but one that will gallop for the foreseeable future as many big institutions –the DoD, Big Banks, Mag 7, UK & Japan, for example—have just started to pull US quantum computing into their processes.

Quantum Computing

Quantum computers rely on qubits (AKA quantum bits) and most systems must operate at temps near absolute zero (say 10 millikelvin) to minimize “environmental noise” and errors.

Helium-3 is essential for achieving these conditions, particularly in the dilution refrigerators used by systems developed by companies like IBM, Google, and IonQ.

Every cryogenic cooling dilution unit needs a few dozen liters of helium-3, and as quantum scales from experimental 10-qubit systems to 100-qubit titans, the demand for the isotope goes up dramatically. In conjunction to this, the transition from a few dozen “science project” machines to thousands globally placed in each big corporation or ministry in the next decade would exponentially expand the required supply for helium-3. The IEA recently even suggested that helium-3 shortages (which have existed off and on since 2006) could hamper quantum computing adoption.

Driving Demand

If Pulsar successfully brings its Topaz helium-3 and helium-4 production online by late 2027, the company will tap into massive demand across quantum computing, chip manufacturing, aerospace, and advanced diagnostics. Its proven natural Helium-3 concentration range of 11.2-11.9 parts per billion (ppb) is astounding and even the more common helium-4 demand is projected to double by 2035.

The base 380 million cubic feet of helium-4 estimated to be at Topaz translates into 4.17 billion liters (1 cubic foot equals 28.31 liters).

Helium has a set of properties that make it indispensable across the space and high-tech manufacturing spheres. Because it is non-reactive, it has long been used to safely fill the empty spaces in rocket tanks without interacting with the volatile fuels themselves. Now that commercial launches are routine –practically one a day— the use of helium-4 is seeing upward pressure. Amazon’s purchase of Globalstar yesterday and Space X’s ambitions for a million-satellite network only point to more future need.

Likewise, helium has become critical in advanced chip manufacture. Its inertness ensures stable fabrication and precise photomask alignment during the lithography phase; its presence prevents oxidation during the etching phase, and –due to its high thermal conductivity and atomic size—it dissipates the heat that would otherwise damage a chip’s nanometer-scale structures.

Scientific research and medical diagnostics have always been a major demand center for helium. When cooled to near absolute zero, liquid helium becomes a superfluid with zero viscosity, an essential characteristic for operating superconducting systems. The CERN Large Hadron Collider’s 27 km ring needs helium to cool its superconducting magnets, as do the 30,000 particle accelerators worldwide.

The same operative principle drives a hospital’s MRI machine. Hospitals are the largest consumers of helium globally, accounting for about 30%-33% of total demand. Each MRI unit typically requires about 1400 liters of liquid helium for cooling, with periodic refills. This exceptional cooling capability is also driving helium’s growing role in modern data centers.

The Opportunity

At present, the Sproule base estimate of 380 million cubic feet of gas (and 2.8 billion in reservoir total) is predicated on Jetstream #1’s well and less than 20% of the acreage. If just another three or four wells confirm similar flow rates, that reservoir number is likely to rise to 3 to 5 billion cubic feet (at a 50% recovery percentile –P50). Assuming even a lowball 7% Helium-4 concentration, we could see a total raw gas volume of 28 to 57 million cubic feet (mcf).

Let’s assume a Helium-4 price of $69.57/mcf (thousand cubic feet), i.e. the April 15th price for North American delivery as stated here, and that Chart Industries has completed the processing plant for $25 million based on four wells.

This facility, processing 10 million cubic feet per day (mcf/d) of raw gas, would generate --assuming a lowball 7% Helium-4 at a 95% recovery-- an annual revenue of $16.65 million. At 12 mcf/d, we are looking at $19.98 million in revenue.

For a more upbeat outlook: let’s assume that processing hits 14 mcf/d with 9% helium by 2028, and He-4 has returned to its recent April 2019 price high of $81.48. That suggests $35.5 million in revenue a year. (Of course, the He-4 price is much higher in Asia right now, where it fetches $167.36 MFC, so there is room above the April 2019 highs).

The numbers for Pulsar start to work. And this doesn’t include Helium-3, which, based on Jetstream #1’s ppb and the present $20 million/kg, could bring in $70 to $90 million a year by 2030 if the demand is there.

Big Risks

Pulsar Helium presents a very high-risk investment profile. It is an early-stage, profitless E&P company with an unproven business model, with serious, ongoing dilution risk, and a maturity wall later in the year.

This is a company that could easily go to zero, especially if “primary helium” is inevitably trumped by “by-product helium.” Over the past two decades, helium has typically arrived on the market as a dross by-product of natural gas extraction. Exxon’s (XOM) LaBarge facility in Wyoming, provides 20% of the world’s helium, and was recently tapped as a critical non-Qatari source of helium-4 that might help mitigate the present crisis.

Becoming a primary helium provider is “niche-y” but maybe dangerous.

For investors, dilution is another danger. I could easily imagine 25%-30% more shares being issued in the 18 months as the firm hits its true build-out phase. And this ties to another issue: the nature of the reserve and the flow rate. Though the drilling locations suggest a pressure consistent with a continuous reservoir, that is presently just a speculation. Things become more expensive if it is not. Geologic eccentricities are the nature of the beast in this industry.

Another very specific risk is that there is a maturity wall which occurs on November 30, 2026. Early last year, that University Bancorp lent Pulsar (via its US subsidiary) a $4 million credit facility at 12% annual interest, which was secured by all of Topaz’s project assets. $2.5 million has been tapped; $1.5 million remains untapped.

If this is not paid, the site as an asset is handed over to University! Now, of course that bank’s CEO is on Pulsar’s board, that bank was an early investor, and more recently it had offered $12.5 million for the facility build-out, but this legal risk still exists and is material.

Conclusion

Because Qatar supplies 33% to 38% of the world’s helium, the Iran War has already started to impact global supplies with the IEA warning that shortages could eventually delay chip manufacture and the impede quantum computing’s rollout.

HD makers like Seagate and Western Digital use helium as it is 7x less dense than air and have reported price increases of 20-30% arriving in April, while South Korean titans like Samsung and SK Hynix have declared only six months of helium inventory before the production must slow.

24% of the present helium supply goes to chip factories, and that number is projected to rise to 30% by 2030. The US Department of Defense recently suggested setting up a 6-month helium reserve (which ironically it had for most of the 20th century), but that would be for future conflicts.

While stock volatility and dilution are likely in the near term, Pulsar Helium actively benefits from the new contours of helium demand in high-tech pursuits, and the geopolitical uncertainties of the Gulf in the long term.

If the geology is cooperative and the build-out expeditious, PSRHF could be generating substantial revenue by 2028-2029 because Topaz stands out as a rare geological asset, with more helium-3 ppb than is found in lunar regolith.

Its accessible location in beautiful, bucolic Minnesota offers a real advantage compared to the more speculative alternatives like Interlune’s planned helium-3 extraction on the Moon.

I see Pulsar as a very speculative buy, but that could easily triple by 2028 if the company succeeds with the right operational follow-through and sees real traction in revenue growth in its first year.

Helium-3 has long been called "Blue Gold." Quantum demand makes its development proposition even more lucrative. Pulsar arguably has the most unique He-3 asset on earth. The question now is whether this tiny micro-cap can deliver on its build-out and bring in customers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: KeyCorp: Q1 Validates The Earnings Growth Thesis
data.description:
Shares of KeyCorp (KEY) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 50%. The stock has been a bit more volatile over the past two months, given uncertainty about the war in Iran and private credit losses. Key has positioned itself well with a stake sale to Scotiabank (BNS), which improved its capital position to enable a balance sheet optimization program. Its relationship has proven so fruitful that BNS is considering upping its stake to 20%, a potential technical support for shares. I last covered Key in January, rating the stock a “Buy,” and shares have gained 2% since then, in keeping with the market. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, KeyCorp earned $0.44 per share, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue jumped 10% to $1.95 billion. EPS was up a third from last year, speaking to the benefits of its balance sheet optimization, which has greatly improved its margins and allowed the company to return to a growth footing.

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposit performance has been muted. As you can see below, deposits were down 2% sequentially and 1% from last year. This year-over-year decline is entirely due to lower brokered CDs, which are the highest-cost type of deposit. Key has excess liquidity on its balance sheet with just a 75% loan-to-deposit ratio (this could safely be 80%), and so it is strategically letting these high-cost balances roll off. Client deposits were up 1% from last year. This is still quite muted, and I classify Key’s deposit performance as “stable” more so than growing. Given its excess liquidity, Key has been aggressive in cutting deposit rates, cutting interest-bearing rates by 22bps sequentially, an ~80% beta to the drop in fed funds during the quarter. This aggressive rate action has likely hampered growth, but it has enhanced margins.

On the asset side of the balance sheet, loans were up 3% from last year to $108 billion; this was also up 5% sequentially. As you can see below, it has been meaningfully shifting its loan mix, letting low-rate consumer loans (namely mortgages) roll off and reinvesting the proceeds into higher-yielding commercial loans. This has kept loan yields from falling more sharply due to Fed rate cuts, with yields down just 12bps from a year ago to 5.35%.

The majority of business loans are to investment-grade borrowers, and its consumer loan portfolio is to borrowers with an excellent 767 FICO score. Now, bank exposure to private credit has been a key focus. About $11 billion, or 10% of its portfolio, is tied to private credit, a relatively high exposure. However, 91% of this portfolio is currently investment grade.

Its primary exposure is lending to funds/counterparties with a pool of private credit exposure where Key has 30%-50% structural protection. This means there could be substantial losses on the underlying pool of private credit loans before Key takes any losses. This is essentially simulating for Key what a AAA CLO is like, and none have ever defaulted. While I would not argue that Key will face zero losses on its private credit exposure, it has structured its portfolio in such a way that losses should be small, barring a severe collapse in the sector (which would likely coincide with a material U.S. recession).

In the past, Key’s earnings have been limited by fixed-rate hedges, locked in when rates were lower. As you can see, though, its swaps decline steadily through the year, and their yield rises, creating a natural benefit to revenue in coming quarters. Next year, it has less hedging at an even higher rate. Beyond this, it has $5 billion of fixed-rate loans maturing this year at 4.2%, which can likely be deployed at 6% or higher. This fixed reinvestment tailwind provides a clear upward trajectory to results.

Net interest income (“NII”) grew 11% from last year to $1.23 billion. Key’s net interest margin (“NIM”) was also up 5bps sequentially to 2.87%, also a 29bp increase from last year. The rolling-off of fixed-rate swaps and lower deposit pricing continue to contribute to wider margins, as does the shift towards commercial lending. With no rate cuts likely before September and these tailwinds continuing, I expect continued margin expansion.

Separately, noninterest income was up $55 million to $723 million. Investment banking was up 13%, and the environment for M&A is very favorable at the moment. I expect that tailwind to continue, which also supports higher debt issuance fees. Its wealth management unit also saw a 13% increase, and the strength in markets should support further AUM growth, leading to incremental revenue over the balance of the year.

Net charge-offs were 38bps in Q1, down 5bps from a year ago. During the quarter, Key set aside $106 million for credit losses, about $5 million more than charge-offs, to accommodate ongoing loan growth. Over the past year, its criticized loans have declined by about $900 million to $5.4 billion, and it has $682 million of nonperforming loans (“NPLs”), or 0.62% of loans. This was down 3bps from a year earlier. It has $1.75 billion of reserves, or 2.56x coverage of NPLs. This is right in line with my 2.5x metric for very strong reserves, leaving KEY well insulated should any unexpected credit issues arise.

Key has a strong capital position with a common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 11.4%. Adjusting for unrealized losses, its capital position is now 10%, which is a very healthy place to operate at. As such, it can return most of its earnings back to shareholders. Indeed, during the quarter, Key repurchased $389 million of stock. Its share count is down by 2% over the past year, and I expect at least a 4% decline in 2026. Shares also offer a 3.8% dividend yield, and this payout is very secure.

Alongside results, management updated guidance. It now expects NII to be at the high end of the prior range, thanks to faster loan growth. Thus far, there has been no impact from the Iran War on loan demand. Still, 2-4% loan growth is not particularly high and is consistent with the low single-digit loan growth I expect from the industry this year. I continue to believe it can achieve at least a 305bp NIM by year's end.

Back in January, I was targeting $1.80-$1.95 of EPS. This drove my $23 price target, which was briefly reached in February. I now expect results to be in the $1.85-$1.95 range, reflecting fewer rate cuts. With its solid capital position and clear earnings growth trajectory from fixed-rate reinvestment, I believe KEY merits a 12x multiple, at the high end of where I value regional banks. This points to shares getting back past $23. This provides a ~9%-10% total return inclusive of the dividend.

While at the low end of my ~10% buy threshold, I am retaining KEY as a "Buy" as I believe it has one of the most credible earnings growth stories, and it has capital even with unrealized losses above where it needs thanks to its past capital injection. This positions KEY relatively well vs. the sector for growth. Additionally, I remain comfortable with credit quality, especially with such strong reserves. I view KEY as a best-in-breed regional and remain a buyer after a solid Q1.

data.title: AOD: Diversified Equity Income For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (AOD) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors diversified equity exposure in dividend-paying companies, focusing on long-term growth. The fund actively targets a rolling distribution rate of 12%, an appealing objective for income-focused investors. With a dynamic sector- & regional-rotation strategy, AOD exhibits flexibility to rotate into growth markets rather than being tied to a thematic approach, enabling the ability to drive value in most market conditions. Given the appeal of the strategy, its historical performance, relatively low management fees, and strong dividend distribution rate, I am recommending AOD with a Buy rating.

Investment Assessment

As of October 31, 2025 in its annual report, AOD was heavily allocated to the information technology sector, making up 25.7% of the total portfolio weight. Within the sector, semiconductor & semiconductor equipment carried the largest subsector exposure in the strategy, which largely consisted of Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO).

Thematically, ADI is positioned to realize a major market turnaround as investments in data centers and associated infrastructure are expected to increase drastically in 2026. A major component is the power requirements of data center infrastructure, requiring onsite base stations and distribution infrastructure to support the substantial electrical needs. This is expected to drive ADI’s power and microcontroller business for the coming years, particularly as data centers transition to 800-VDC beginning in 2027.

Broadcom is also in an appealing position as hyperscalers transition more capital investments towards custom silicon solutions, delivering higher performance to the firm. Long-term, Broadcom is positioned to deliver multiple gigawatts of capacity over the coming years, resulting in sharper growth for its semiconductor solutions business. In addition to custom ASICs, Broadcom provides networking infrastructure necessary to connect the XPUs and GPUs as well as to interconnect data center sites for the “scale up” and “scale out” theme.

The second largest sector concentration is in financials, making up 16% of the total portfolio weight. AOD largely holds the megabanks like Bank of America Corporation (BAC), The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), as well as non-bank financial institutions like Blackstone Inc. (BX) and CME Group Inc. (CME). The financials sector is in an interesting position as interest rate policy has been put on hold as renewed inflation fears return following the beginning of the war in Iran. Accordingly, headline inflation jumped to 3.3% in March 2026 driven by higher energy costs, including gasoline, fuel oil, and gas. Core inflation was much more moderate at a rate of 2.6%, creating some uncertainty in the market.

On the one hand, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has amplified energy scarcity, particularly across the Asian markets that depend on Middle Eastern oil and LNG imports. On the other hand, negotiations are underway with energy markets whiplashing in an attempt to find a base. With incremental optimism towards war negotiations, energy prices have become more moderate following months of elevated prices. If prices continue to ease, inflationary concerns may abate, allowing the Federal Reserve to continue the rate-easing cycle. If interest rates were to be reduced later in 2026, banks could benefit from greater deal flow, particularly for debt issuances and refinancing. AOD largely sat out the energy price spike with only 4.4% sector exposure.

AOD provides diversified regional exposure with the US accounting for roughly 62% of regional weight; France, the Netherlands, and the UK each account for roughly 5% of the portfolio weight. Regional rotation can be an appealing feature for investors seeking a more diversified portfolio strategy, particularly when considering diversified industrials, materials, and technology.

For example, AOD invests in semi-cap equipment manufacturer ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), which develops EUV technology for advanced semiconductor manufacturing, as well as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation Limited (TSM), an advanced chip fabricator as part of the global semiconductor supply chain.

Despite the early-2026 headwinds the portfolio faced, I believe AOD can provide an appealing strategy for growth and income over the long-run. Using the iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI) as a proxy for AOD’s benchmark, the fund has largely outperformed in recent years, presenting an appealing investment outlay for those seeking growth & income.

From an investment perspective, AOD trades at an appealing discount to NAV when compared to its historical trading rate. Essentially, investors can own a portfolio positioned for growth at a -3.36% discount, an appealing dispersion when compared to holding the positions outright. This may provide investors with an appealing NAV arbitrage strategy, particularly if growth in the equity holdings drive more investments into the strategy, potentially bringing it closer to NAV. Holding AOD at the current discount provides a 0.39% upside in the distribution relative to NAV, an appealing dispersion for investors seeking to maximize income through equity exposure.

About abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund

AOD was launched by aberdeen Investments as a closed-end fund on January 26, 2007 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The fund has a relatively low net expense ratio of 118bps, making it an appealing portfolio option when considering the robust distribution policy and historical performance. The fund presently has $1.10 billion in assets under management; as of February 2026, the fund applied $2.8 million in leverage, a negligible amount when considering the total portfolio and associated fees.

AOD pays out monthly income with an annualized forward distribution rate of $1.32/share with a forward yield of 13.12%. Investors must consider that roughly 60%+ of the 2025 distributions were a return of capital and this may continue through 2026.

Looking at total returns, AOD has performed exceptionally well when compared to peer equity income strategies. While the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) remains an appealing, low-cost alternative, investors should weigh whether they’re seeking global or solely US equity exposure. In a similar respect to AOD, ADX has a minimum distribution rate of 6%, though has significantly surpassed its minimum in the last 5 years.

Risks Related to AOD

AOD is an actively managed, closed-end fund designed to provide investors with global equity exposure, presenting certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. CEFs typically provide limited visibility into current holdings, meaning that exposure in the most recent financial reports may be using dated information. Investors will be exposed to manager risk, relying on the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight securities in the fund. Given the flexible nature of the fund, I believe there may be greater risk to asset allocation as AOD isn’t sector- or regionally-focused.

Final Thoughts

AOD can be an appealing equity income investment strategy for those seeking an actively managed portfolio strategy. With an appealing distribution rate and management fees, AOD presents an investment opportunity to gain diversified equity exposure across various regions. Given the historical performance, strong distribution, and low fees, I am recommending AOD with a Buy rating.

data.title: IVES: A New Stage In The AI Tech Revolution
data.description:
Tech stocks have been on fire lately. And I mean all of tech, from mega-caps to SMID caps, from semis to software. It's too early to say whether a new AI revolution is unfolding, one that benefits infrastructure names, enablers, and users, but there’s certainly a degree of optimism permeating what was once a beleaguered group.

Is a new AI-renaissance moment at hand? It's possible. For that, the Dan IVES Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (IVES) is a solid proxy. The 30-stock portfolio has performed very well since I initiated coverage on it with a "Buy" rating last July. IVES has returned 17%, dividends included, compared to a 12% S&P 500 gain. After a tumultuous October-March stretch, I assert that a new rally is now underway in April and beyond. So, I keep with a "Buy" rating.

IVES: Outperforming QQQ Since Inception

According to the issuer, IVES seeks to capitalize on the rapid growth of artificial intelligence (AI) by investing in companies poised to lead the AI transformation. This fund offers investors exposure to a diversified portfolio of firms at the cutting edge of AI technology.

IVES has grown substantially since last July. Its total assets under management is now just shy of $1 billion. It hasn’t taken off to the extent that Tom Lee’s Granny Shots ETF (GRNY) has, but it has accomplished its objectives, in my view.

The annual expense ratio is what most investors may point at as to why IVES is not the ideal tech-revolution ETF. At 75 basis points, it’s upwards of 0.7 percentage points more than you’d pay for a straightforward broad index ETF (like Nasdaq 100 ETFs). So, I absolutely acknowledge the high cost. I’d like to see IVES dip into more up-and-coming tech themes, due to Dan Ives’ and Webush’s expertise in this area. I will keep monitoring that.

As it stands, share-price momentum appears strong, given the recent snap-back rally. While it is not yet graded by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, I’ll call out later that the RSI momentum gauge is at a multi-month high.

Elsewhere, IVES has been shown to be a risky product. I presumed the ETF would see notable drawdowns if a tech correction took place, and we certainly got that. The fund dropped from $35 to $27, a 24% plunge from October to late March. Shares are higher by nearly 20% just since the March 30 low.

On liquidity, IVES is strong. Average daily volume is more than 600,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just three basis points.

For an update on the portfolio, the ETF is not yet rated by Morningstar, but it plots along the very top row of the style box, indicating its exposure to U.S. large-cap growth. Still, 53% of IVES is allocated away from that style box area, while 16% of the fund houses ex-U.S. stocks. So, there’s more diversification than you might think.

The valuation remains reasonable, too. The current price-to-earnings ratio is 23.2x, which is close to that of the Nasdaq 100, while the long-term EPS growth rate is high at 15.7%. The resulting PEG ratio is only barely above 1.5x, which is a decent GARP pricing.

IVES: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, Information Technology, not surprisingly, commands a high 71% of the ETF. Consumer Discretionary is the next-largest stake at 13% (close to a market weight). There is no resource exposure (such as Energy and Materials).

IVES’ sector makeup hurt the product late last year and throughout much of 2026’s first quarter. But with TMT and the Mag 7 now leading, it’s a tailwind.

IVES: Holdings & Dividend Information

As far as seasonality is concerned, I pulled up QQQ’s 15-year performance heat map by month. Here, we find that big-cap tech is generally in a bullish calendar phase.

Gains have been strong, on average, now through July.

QQQ Seasonality: Bullish Through July

The Technical Take

With strong absolute performance (the October-March correction notwithstanding), IVES’ technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares are up in 10 of the last 11 sessions and nearly 20% above the March 30 intraday nadir.

The long-term 200-day moving average is now seen, and it’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Price is also above the 200dma and 50dma, which is encouraging. I’d like to see a bullish golden cross pattern play out soon, though.

But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. Near 70, it’s at the best level going back to the October 29 high in price. While shares must still contend with a downtrend resistance line, I assert that there’s enough technical momentum for IVES to eventually reach $34 resistance (the January high). To that point, the ETF rallied through a downtrend resistance line from February to early April, just in the past two trading days.

IVES: Bullish False Breakdown, Rising 200dma, Strong RSI Momentum

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on IVES. The fund is not as cheap as a basic Nasdaq 100 Index ETF, but its performance has been strong since inception. It has beaten QQQ by three percentage points back to July last year, and I’ll be watching for what changes Dan Ives makes with the allocation as the tech revolution enters a new stage.

data.title: Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund Q1 2026 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

Notable Contributors & Detractors

Boston Beer (SAM) – Alcoholic beverage company Boston Beer was a contributor for the quarter as industry data improved compared to last year. Sentiment around the industry appears to be leveling off after two years of headwinds from changes in consumption habits. The company reported solid first quarter results that were highlighted by continued improvement in gross margins. Recent innovation Sun Cruiser is growing volumes rapidly as the brand expands nationwide to take advantage of the sizable runway remaining in the ready-to-drink tea space. The company also continues to make progress in fixing problems at Twisted Tea, their largest brand. Boston Beer remains both an active share repurchaser and attractive acquisition target as alcohol industry consolidation looks to be on the horizon.

Shenandoah Telecommunications (SHEN) – Telecom company Shenandoah was a contributor for the quarter. The company reported a solid set of results that helped the market wake up to these off-the-radar assets that we started buying last year. While parts of this unique company are technically “legacy” telecom, Shenandoah made the wise call to focus on building out its fiber business years ago. This led to decreased short-term earnings power and a higher leverage ratio on reported EBITDA, but the company built a substantial amount of value with this capital allocation decision. Now a strong majority of its value comes from this great, growing business. Shenandoah also wisely securitized most of its fiber assets in the 4th quarter to improve the balance sheet. The company is now getting closer to FCF positive in 2027, and earnings growth should continue to be strong thereafter.

Empire State Realty (ESRT) and Alexander’s (ALX) – We wrote about these two New York real estate companies together in a previous note and will now do so again. Their stock prices moved in different directions this quarter. To the positive, Alexander’s announced a great sale of a non-core, non-income-generating asset in Queens that puts the company further on offense. While ESRT is also actively looking to sell assets, they did not have any news on that front in the quarter and therefore declined with other real estate companies as fears about AI-driven job losses hit the sector. International travel trends also continued to impact their Observatory business. In spite of these headwinds, ESRT reported solid leasing results and should be able to grow cash flow this year. Both companies still have irreplaceable assets driven by aligned partners. Their divergent stock price moves led to us trimming Alexander’s and adding to ESRT as the quarter went on.

Rayonier (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) – Timberland companies Rayonier and PotlatchDeltic completed their merger in the quarter to become our largest single position in the Fund. While the combination of the pre-merger-close price move up in PotlatchDeltic and the post-merger-close price move down in Rayonier did not combine to make this a large detractor, it is worth noting the post-merger weakness we believe did not line up with the solid value creation opportunity at a company like this. While some of the sell-off was likely short-term/technical (index rebalancing), timberland remains a great and long-term store of value. Interest rate moves and housing market sentiment can impact perception in the short term, but this asset class has historically been an inflation hedge that has performed well in a variety of environments. Post-merger, Rayonier has a strong balance sheet and the ability to go on offense. We believe that targeted asset sales and share repurchase can drive value per share growth from here. We also wrote about the company in our most recent Research Perspectives note .

Becle (BCCLF) – Spirits company Becle detracted after the company reported a quarter that the stock market did not like. This short-term earnings volatility (on a business that is 200+ years old) was driven by a shift in distribution that is the right thing to do for the long-term. While this means that reported revenues will be down a few percent in 2026, this sets the company up for growth in 2027 and beyond. As mentioned above on Boston Beer, industry trends did not get worse, but there remains debate about spirits consumption going forward. The biggest industry news came late in the quarter when it was confirmed that Pernod Ricard and Brown-Forman were discussing a merger. The industry is ripe for consolidation, and now this could be kicking off. Becle will be poised to act from a position of financial strength while being focused on the still fastest growing part of the spirits industry, tequila. We added to our position in the quarter after the stock declined.

Mattel (MAT) – Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a detractor in the quarter. The stock fell due to an unexpected $150 million (15% of EBITDA) in incremental spending on initiatives including mobile gaming, Brick Shop (Mattel's competitor to LEGO), and direct to consumer marketing. CEO Ynon Kreiz cited a one-year payback on this spend, but the market remains in show-me mode and the price declined proportionate to the 2026 earnings per share guidance reduction. This was compounded by 4Q results that missed expectations, especially in the US. To the positive, the company committed to $1.5 billion in share repurchase over the next 3 years, which equates to 33% of shares outstanding at today's price. 2026 should have demonstrated the true FCF power of Mattel given traction on IP initiatives including two new movies (Masters of the Universe and Matchbox), two new mobile game launches, and licensing momentum with Toy Story 5 and KPop Demon Hunters. Unfortunately, this was delayed, and we are focused on what we can do to improve this situation.

Tripadvisor (TRIP) – Digital experiences, dining and travel company Tripadvisor was a detractor for the quarter. As previously mentioned, we were pleased to see board improvement at the company. Unfortunately, this came after a disappointing quarter and a lack of board urgency up to this point. The legacy Tripadvisor business has been worse than we first thought a year ago. However, it has gone from only ~20% of our value then to now closer to 10%, while Viator and TheFork have grown. Macro travel trends got worse in the quarter as well. To the positive, we believe that the company is now acting on what is important to realize value per share. There are a variety of strategic options available to this company, and we look forward to how this year develops.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter we had no new purchases or exits. We remain disciplined in our search for businesses that meet our Business, People, and Price criteria and are close to buying multiple new investments that you could hear about next quarter.

Before investing in any Longleaf Partners Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For a current Prospectus and Summary Prospectus, which contain this and other important information, visit https://regdocs.blugiant.com/longleaf/# . Please read the Prospectus and Summary Prospectus carefully before investing.

Risks

The Longleaf Small-Cap Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Smaller company stocks may be more volatile with less financial resources than those of larger companies.

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 3000 represents approximately 97% of the American public equity market and the Russell 2000 represents 10% of the Russell 3000. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a Fund and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

Enterprise value (EV) is a company's market capitalization plus debt, minority interest and preferred shares, and less total cash and cash equivalents.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

REIT is a Real Estate Investment Trust which is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate.

A Basis Point is one hundredth of one percent.

Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) are business transactions in which the ownership of a company, business organization, or one of their operating units is transferred to or consolidated with another entity.

As of March 31, 2026, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund: Rayonier, Inc. 8.1%; Becle S.A.B. de C.V. 7.6%; GCI Liberty, Inc. 7.5%; Tripadvisor, Inc. 7.0%; Mattel, Inc. 6.8%; Shenandoah Telecommunications Company 6.5%; CNX Resources Corporation 5.6%; Empire State Realty Trust, Inc. 4.8%; White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd. 4.8% and Graham Holdings Company 4.8%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Funds distributed by ALPS Distributors, Inc.

LLP001631 expires 7/15/2026

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This V-Bottom Rally Could Be Quickly Forming Another Peak
data.description:
Market Turned From Extreme Bearish To Extreme Bullish?

The market is now at an all-time high, even though the war hasn’t yet quite reached a definitive conclusion. Does it surprise you?

If anything, I think it goes to prove that that's just how the forward looking market really works.

Sure, we have not reached a permanent ceasefire. But it's not like it really matters to the market right now.

Especially not when you see the market reverse into new highs from a bottom just about 2 weeks ago.

The market seems to be telling us that the whatever happens in the Middle East no longer matters that much once we are firmly past the worst stages of it.

Has it also stunned the media? You bet. But, it's not like the market really cares, right?

Markets Don't Need 100% Clarity To Turn Bullish

I thought this commentary by Goldman Sachs (GS) aptly summarized what the market is thinking versus what the media is worried about.

The media wants a clear conclusion, but experienced investors should be able to tell us that the market had already discounted them in late March.

It’s arguably true that there are still a laundry list of uncertainties that could really find their way back into the narrative later.

But, it’s also crucial for us to recognize that the market doesn’t quite need absolute clarity to regain control of the bullish skew, right?

Think about it. In the absolute mess of the COVID supply chain snafu, the market continued to rally until it became a tech and growth bubble. But, that only happened like when? As a reminder, that was late 2021.

And then last year’s tariffs. It was only earlier this year that President Trump’s tariffs were struck down by SCOTUS. Although, there could be ways around it to get them reinstated by July, as Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent opined recently.

Yet, the market rallied for an incredible six month period for growth and tech through late 2025, before the reallocation themes then occurred as investors switched focus from growth to value.

But, that value narrative then took a steep tumble (sans energy stocks), as the Middle Eastern conflict unfolded.

However, even as the ceasefire negotiations continue hanging over the horizon, as parties negotiate to extend them, the market is already looking past it.

Did investors get it right?

But Being Extremely Bullish Too Quickly Is Also A Problem

A V-bottom is a very powerful event.

There’s no doubt that CTAs went into the April bottom at nearly max bearish, as seen above.

The metric appears to have flipped completely to the upside as I pen this write-up, which corroborates the V-bottom market print that we observed.

With such an extreme positioning into the April lows, the sudden narrative shift likely also caused them to go long rapidly, spurring the momentum spike that we are seeing now.

Market strategists have also gone bullish, generally speaking.

And during this time, Wall Street hasn't even downgraded the market's earnings growth outlook.

I guess it shows how confident the analysts have been on the underlying strength of the companies in the market.

And it seems like nothing could quite unhinge the AI infrastructure buildout themes that have driven this broad-based recovery.

But, given how quickly the recovery has panned out, it's also sensible for us to assess whether such a positioning shift has also been taken out of context already?

Also, on the CTAs. As the flows have shifted into extreme positioning, we must now consider whether a slowdown is due, or potentially even reverse?

Moreover, as these extreme bearish positions get unwound so quickly, it has also translated into the market print, right?

These effects are also shown clearly in the collapse of the liquidity premium in just the past two weeks. If anything, we now have clear confirmation that the market has moved resoundingly back into the risk-on mode.

V-Bottom Prints Are Usually Strong Bottoms

Normally, I’m in favor of V-bottoms. We have had a few notable prints in the past ten years.

The COVID one in March/April of 2020 was a clear example. And last year’s Liberation Day print was another one to remember.

I think the market has been proven right again with how the 2026 bottom formed.

Nonetheless, there are many things to worry about; I'm not saying things are crystal clear now.

These include how the supply chain will rejiggle itself, as we don’t quite have clear answers into the shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz with the naval blockade.

Former IMF Chief Economist & Harvard University Professor Kenneth Rogoff made the case for us to consider that we may not quite be prepared for worse shocks that could be making their way into the economy.

Next, we also don’t quite know whether the unemployment related to AI will ultimately have a more pernicious effect on consumer spending.

However, based on recent studies, that doesn’t seem to be the case yet.

Although these are really rapidly evolving dynamics, and I think it is worth closely watching.

While the results from Wall Street’s leading banks show that consumer spending stays robust, these changes might not have been captured into the future outlook if the unemployment rate continues to worsen.

And we already see how companies like Block (XYZ) and now Snap (SNAP) are using AI as their primary motive to slash headcounts.

Companies could be emboldened to use the AI narrative to get in shape, and we don't quite yet know how pervasive these things could get. But, it's definitely worth watching.

But A Topping Signal Could Also Be Forthcoming

And then, price action.

My bullish undertones from the start of April have now quickly shifted to a more neutral leaning, as the market came back much quicker than I had anticipated.

The print is also starting to flash caution for me, because it might indicate that the extreme bearish positioning has given way to extreme bullish positioning.

I’m not saying that this presages the next bear market, but it also indicates that the market has quickly thrown the tail risk scenarios out of the window too quickly. Does that make sense?

To some extent, yes. The conflict is likely past the worst stages, I believe.

However, to say that there wouldn’t be any effects and think that the market shouldn’t discount supply chain risks, especially relating to the shipping flows through the Strait, doesn't sound reasonable to me.

A slower and steadier recovery would have been the much more preferred advancing move.

That allows the market more time to digest the developments.

This stark shift from very bearish to very bullish has arguably taken the narratives way too far all of a sudden.

At this point, the market looks like it wants to get that decisive breakout, but I’m not convinced.

Until I see it print, if you managed to get in very aggressively in the first two weeks of April, this will be the moment where I will urge you to hold back your horses.

There will be a time to get back aggressively again, but not unless the market shows that it isn’t a double top bearish reversal print in the making.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ProPetro: A Re-Rating Gone Too Far, Too Fast
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Oil remains unstable as Iran and U.S. tensions continue to push the prices up and down with each headline. Under normal conditions, this arrangement would provide oilfield services with some support.

However, this cycle is different as U.S. shale producers are not chasing growth anymore. They concentrate on the capital discipline and free cash flow rather than volume. The change has disrupted the connection between oil prices and service demand.

This brings me to ProPetro Holding (PUMP) which is a pretty intriguing case.

ProPetro operates in the Permian Basin and earns most of its revenue from hydraulic fracturing. In simple words, it helps other companies extract oil and gas from underground.

Additionally, its management is attempting to develop a second growth leg by PROPWR. This segment basically targets power generation and infrastructure contracts of longer duration.

Moreover, its Q4 results were pretty mixed.

Revenue clocked in at $290 million, a decrease of about 9-10% annually. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA was close to $51 million, with margin of around 18%.

Therefore, the activity is still soft. Furthermore, the earnings beat appeared solid but it was rather due to efficiency gains than to actual demand recovery.

Interestingly, PUMP shares have skyrocketed over 175% YOY (as of writing), but 2026 expectations of EPS remain a little negative at approximately -$0.13.

The market is obviously beginning to price in a shift towards a more infrastructure-linked business model.

In general, that is the core tension for me. The business is progressing, yet earnings are yet to follow. This gap ends up justifying my Hold rating at the current levels.

Core Business: Cash Flow Strong, Growth Absent

ProPetro is using its traditional fracturing business as a cash cow. It is being used to squeeze out as much money as possible to pay for a future in power.

The above snapshot is interesting. We can clearly see that the company’s revenue has consistently decreased throughout the year.

Let’s put this into perspective. In Q1 25, its revenue came in at $359 million which decreased around 20% to $290 million in Q4.

This means that the U.S. shale producers have diverted their focus from just drilling aggressively. They are now more interested in creating FCF and returning capital to shareholders.

This change effectively caps the demand for ProPetro’s traditional services. The "drill at any cost" era is over and the company’s ability to hike prices is over with it, too.

Given the declining revenue, the 18% EBITDA margin is a huge win. It shows that ProPetro has perfected the art of cost discipline. The management is pushing into its Permian dominance to extract even the last penny out of already existing fleets.

The actual disconnection is that it generated $232 million in operating cash flow for FY 25 but only $1 million in net income. The money is real, whereas the accounting profits are low due to extensive depreciation and high expense of maintaining old equipment. It is a company that generates cash but cannot present the profits on paper.

What is more important is that it is no longer an independent growth narrative. The management is shifting capital to PROPWR.

The FY 26 total capex guidance is a massive $390-$435 million. Out of this, a whopping $250-$275 million is assigned to the power segment while trimming traditional completions spending to $140-$160 million.

That said, internal cash flow alone is not enough to bridge the needs. ProPetro has also raised about $163 million by issuing 15 million shares at a price of roughly $10. This has added dilution while helping in expansion needs.

I think this validates the fact that the core business is stable and cash-generative, however, its mission has changed to fund the transformation.

PROPWR: The Growth Driver Behind the Narrative

The market is now viewing PROPWR like the "golden goose" that justifies every bit of the 160% YOY rally.

PUMP’s power segment already has 240 MW of committed capacity, and plans to expand to more than 1 GW by 2030.

This is a straight play on the huge electrification of the Permian and the increasing data center demand. It is a brilliant twist because it converts a cyclical service company into a quasi-utility.

The economics of this are higher compared to the frac world. We are considering long-term, recurring contracts which do not change with each $5 change in oil. Consequently, potential EBITDA per MW is much greater in comparison to traditional margins.

This is what investors are seeking in terms of better quality earnings. The demand is structural and permanent, as opposed to a momentary stage in the cycle.

However, let’s be real about the execution. The market is already pricing in huge success, but project remains in its initial phases. Alongside, the management is committing to a capital spending of $390-$435 million to get there. Before the cash starts coming back, that is a lot of money to spend.

The other significant layer is the way in which this growth is being funded. ProPetro is also using external equipment financing, such as working with Caterpillar Financial Services.

It brings PROPWR closer to an asset-backed infrastructure model. In this kind of structure, growth occurs at the equipment level instead of being completely backed by company’s balance sheet.

Though it should be noted that this does not eliminate core execution risk. The real outcome is still determined by the demand, utilization, and contract economics.

Valuation Discussion

PUMP stock ballooned a mind boggling ∼178% over the last twelve months. Nevertheless, it did not go up without a hitch.

The upswing was largely driven by a sharp breakout in late 2025, which pushed the stock into the 13-14 range.

The simple money has been made as the market has already digested the power pivot theme.

The stock is trading very close to its 52-week high of $15. It resembles a very optimistic set-up with less upside available.

Let’s see where the mismatch exists. The 2026 consensus earnings per share are estimated at negative 0.13. The company is still likely to record accounting losses despite the stock trading at multi-year highs.

Apparently, this weakness is already being looked through by the market to 2027. This still leaves a prominent gap in expectations that is quite risky.

In effect, investors are buying back their recovery, two years down the road. Any failure in execution, be it due to supply chain problems or slower electrification, could soon strain the valuation.

Moreover, the stock is not cheap at a forward P/S of 1.36x. Indeed, this is due to the uncertainty surrounding the transition and timing of earnings recovery.

Now, let’s assess some scenarios.

The bear case will result in the stock going down to the $8-$10 range. This occurs when the legacy frac business decelerates at a greater pace than PROPWR can scale. It is also more probable when elevated expenditure consumes cash before PROPWR begins to produce decent returns. Then, further dilution would be required, which would damage the valuation at hand.

The base case maintains the stock at $12-$15. In this case, the legacy business remains strong to finance the transition. At the same time, the market waits for 2027 earnings to come through. This remains a ‘show me’ story. The valuation is kept at a limit until backlog is converted into real earnings.

In contrast, the bull case points to $18 or higher. This would require a non-oilfield win of a massive magnitude. It would also refocus valuation to be more of a utility-based multiple versus a service multiple of close to 4x EBITDA.

The risk-reward appears to be stretched overall. Mistakes can be very costly at present levels. Investors are investing in future performance instead of the recent results.

Key Points to Watch and Takeaway

The upcoming Q1 26 earnings report will be a crucial checkpoint.

In case legacy frac revenue is notably less than anticipated, it would begin to undermine the notion that this segment can reliably finance the transition.

Meanwhile, it will be interesting to see how much cash is already pumped into PROPWR.

Then, the emphasis would naturally draw towards execution which remains a central risk.

The current valuation already values successful scaling. Hence, reduced momentum in deployments or slower contract ramp would soon become a load on sentiment.

That is directly related to capital intensity. In case the project costs drift out of control or the project needs some extra funding, transition may be more dilution-intensive. It might also push a meaningful contribution to earnings after 2027.

This risk is even more apparent when we see that 550 MW of equipment has already been ordered.

Lastly, sentiment is also closely related to the AI and power demand theme. In case the expectations regarding data center or Permian electrification falter, the stock would lose its premium. It may then revert to a more conservative oilfield services valuation.

It is easy to be carried away by the excitement when a stock rises over 2.75x within a year.

ProPetro is an incredible story, and the shift to power infrastructure is what the Permian wants. But you need not tie the quality of the idea and the price of the stock together. At $15 per share, you are paying an enormous premium on a transformation that is yet to be tested.

The company is cashflow positive but is technically weak in terms of profitability, which becomes a very thin tightrope.

All in all, for those who are already invested, keep your positions. Concurrently, those who want a piece of PUMP for their portfolios should wait for a better entry point.
